text.title: RSpec Testing Patterns Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules for effective RSpec usage.

rule.statement: Specs MUST use the `describe SUT { context 'when X' { it 'does Y' } }` hierarchy. Bare `it` blocks without context or top-level `describe` are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Hierarchy mirrors BDD: subject + state + behavior; failure output reads as a sentence.

rule.statement: Unit specs MUST use `build_stubbed(:resource)` (in-memory) by default. `create(:resource)` (DB write) MUST be limited to integration/request specs where persistence is verified.
rule.rationale: build_stubbed is 10-100x faster than create; using create everywhere makes the suite minutes-long.

rule.statement: Service specs MUST run without `rails_helper` (use `spec_helper`). Collaborators MUST be passed as mocks; no DB hits.

rationale.source: RSpec Rails reference — request specs vs unit specs

rule.rationale: Isolated specs are fast + focused; coupling service tests to Rails boot drags the suite.

rule.statement: Cross-class invariants (e.g. 'all auditable models have a created_by') MUST live in `shared_examples_for ...` blocks; per-class repetition is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Shared examples document the invariant + apply uniformly + fail when invariants drift.

rule.statement: `let!` (eager) MUST only be used when the setup is required regardless of what's tested. Default `let` (lazy) MUST be used for setup referenced in some specs.
rule.rationale: Eager let! creates unnecessary fixtures, slowing the suite; lazy let triggers only when accessed.
